primary_diagnosis.name: Traumatic corneal epithelial defect from high-velocity particulate injury, with possible retained microscopic corneal foreign body

pathophysiology_summary: High-velocity particulate from power sanding without eye protection has struck the ocular surface, shearing away an area of corneal epithelium and exposing the richly innervated sub-basal nerve plexus — producing severe pain, a foreign-body sensation on each blink, reflex tearing, blepharospasm and photophobia. A microscopic corneal or subtarsal foreign body may be retained. The exposed stroma is a portal for microbial keratitis, which can progress to a corneal ulcer and permanent scarring if not treated with prophylactic topical antibiotic and re-epithelialisation. The critical unresolved question is whether the same high-velocity mechanism has caused a full-thickness (penetrating/open-globe) injury or an intraocular foreign body — neither of which can be excluded without slit-lamp examination, fluorescein/Seidel testing and, if indicated, imaging.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe unilateral pain, foreign-body sensation, tearing and photophobia immediately after high-velocity particulate exposure.
Same mechanism; a microscopic particle may be lodged on the cornea or under the upper lid, reproducing the abrasion sensation on each blink.
High-velocity mechanism can perforate the globe; must be actively excluded in person before an abrasion is assumed.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the mechanism should already flag open-globe injury as the must-not-miss even though a simple abrasion is most likely.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic tetrad — severe ocular pain, reduced visual acuity, photophobia, foreign-body sensation — after mechanical ocular trauma.
Reduced visual acuity after high-velocity trauma keeps globe injury live; telehealth cannot exclude it.
Also causes severe unilateral pain and reduced vision, but expected nausea/vomiting and haloes are absent and there is a clear traumatic mechanism.
Sudden traumatic onset, unilateral severe pain and absence of a URTI prodrome or bilateral spread argue strongly against it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Angle-closure and viral conjunctivitis are the note's stated differentials; they are screened out on history but the AI should not use their exclusion to downgrade the need for in-person globe assessment.

differential.evidence_basis: A large area of fluorescein uptake under cobalt-blue light confirms an epithelial defect; a negative Seidel test excludes globe perforation.
Only slit-lamp, Seidel testing and (if indicated) imaging can definitively exclude this — the reason in-person assessment is mandatory.

differential_progression.scoring_note: These investigations require ED/eye-casualty equipment; they are not achievable in the telehealth encounter itself.

differential.diagnosis: Traumatic corneal abrasion with retained-foreign-body risk, open-globe injury excluded in person
differential.evidence_basis: Most consistent with the whole picture once an in-person examination has excluded perforation and removed any retained particle.

red_flags.description: Reduced/blurred visual acuity in the affected eye after ocular trauma
red_flags.clinical_significance: Vision loss after trauma signals a potentially sight-threatening injury and mandates urgent in-person ophthalmic assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed globe injury or deep abrasion can progress to permanent visual impairment.

red_flags.description: High-velocity particulate mechanism (power sanding without eye protection)
red_flags.clinical_significance: High-velocity mechanism can perforate the globe or drive an intraocular foreign body; it converts a 'minor scratch' into a must-exclude open-globe scenario.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undiagnosed open-globe injury or intraocular foreign body leads to endophthalmitis and irreversible vision loss.

red_flags.description: Signs of a penetrating (open-globe) injury — teardrop/peaked pupil, prolapsed uveal tissue, hyphaema — cannot be excluded via telehealth
red_flags.clinical_significance: These are the specific signs that distinguish a full-thickness injury from a surface abrasion; they require a slit-lamp and cannot be assessed remotely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A ruptured globe managed as an abrasion (drops, patching, pressure) can extrude intraocular contents and be lost.

red_flags.description: A dark curtain/shadow across the vision or new flashes and floaters (retinal detachment) developing after the injury
red_flags.clinical_significance: A late warning sign of retinal detachment or intraocular complication requiring immediate emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed retinal detachment repair causes permanent vision loss.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The tetrad is classic, but the high-velocity mechanism means an occult open-globe injury or intraocular foreign body must be excluded in person before the diagnosis is called simple.

Prescribing or recommending ongoing topical anaesthetic (tetracaine/amethocaine) for pain relief causes corneal epithelial toxicity, delays healing and masks a deteriorating injury — it is for single clinician-administered examination use only.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Pressure patching a possibly perforated globe
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Firm or pressure patching (or any manoeuvre that raises intraocular pressure) can extrude intraocular contents if the globe is actually ruptured — shield, do not pad-and-pressure.
Topical steroids over an epithelial defect with a possible retained organic (wood) particle risk infective keratitis and corneal melt and are contraindicated without ophthalmology direction.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Fluorescein staining and cobalt-blue confirmation of a corneal epithelial defect
Slit-lamp assessment of the cornea and anterior chamber (ciliary flush, cell/flare, hyphaema)
Seidel test to exclude globe perforation
Eyelid eversion to find a retained subtarsal foreign body
Intraocular pressure and pupillary examination for a teardrop/peaked pupil
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day referral to an emergency department or eye-casualty with slit-lamp and fluorescein; shield (not pressure-patch) the eye, advise against rubbing, and arrange someone else to drive.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Anchoring on 'just a corneal scratch' risks missing an occult open-globe injury or intraocular foreign body from the high-velocity mechanism — telehealth cannot exclude these, so the safe action is urgent in-person assessment.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP / Australian therapeutic guidance — corneal abrasion and corneal foreign body
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Topical antibiotic prophylaxis for corneal abrasion; urgent referral where a foreign body cannot be removed, vision is reduced, or penetrating injury is possible; topical anaesthetics are never dispensed for home analgesia.
High-velocity ocular injury requires exclusion of open-globe injury and intraocular foreign body; shield the eye and refer for slit-lamp assessment rather than patching.
